comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Once again, it's technically Monday now -- past midnight Eastern time -- and here I am on Dreamwidth, so it's time for another Magic Monday.  (The picture is Dion Fortune, by the way -- the author of The Cosmic Doctrine, among other things.)

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***

comments.title: Astral Affects of Sleeping and Love
comments.content:
How sharing a bed affects the astral light of both parties? Does this apply to children, or are the shielded?

Is there a analog to "You imitate what you hate" for love? Do we imitate what we love as well? How you would you fill in the blank for "You ___ what you love."

Do you think psychedelics have a place in magical practice? Forgive me if I am stepping on your tail. I recall Alan Moore saying he started his with them and eventually found they were unnecessary.

replies.title: Re: Astral Affects of Sleeping and Love
replies.content:
1) Depends on whether "sharing a bed" includes sexual contact or not. If not, there's a certain attunement of energies, simply because you tend to resonate with the energy of anyone when your aura and theirs are in close proximity, and sleeping within a few feet of each other every night will do that. Children are certainly affected by it, and so are pets.

If sex is involved, that takes things to a much more intensive level. When two people make love, if both are aroused and especially if there's love involved, there's normally some degree of fusion between their auras. When the auras divide again, during the "afterglow," some of each partner's energies end up staying in the other's aura, so you get a much more dramatic degree of attunement and mutual resonance.

2) The maxim I was taught is "What you contemplate, you imitate." That works no matter what your emotional state is when you contemplate. When you hate someone, your attention is fixed on them, and so you start to imitate them; when you love someone, ditto; admiration does the same thing, and so do all the other emotions that focus your attention on the other person.

3) No. Dion Fortune's famous comment still applies: drugs may loosen the girders of the mind but they don't tighten them up afterwards, and unless you want to go through life rattling like a cheap automobile, using psychedelics isn't a good idea. I dabbled in psychedelics back in the early 1980s, but found that I could get much more interesting places using nonchemical means.

replies.title: Re: Astral Affects of Sleeping and Love
replies.content:
JMG and All:

1)The maxim I was taught is "What you contemplate, you imitate."

This reminds me of useful know-how presented in a podcasted interview of Stephen Flowers that I listened to. In it he emphasized that one acts in accord with a general motto of "good thoughts, good words, and good deeds" not to supplicate any superior being but to become symmetrical with them.

With that in mind I think about stories in today's video games and behold a wasteland of perverted ideals. Yikes... Long live Lord of the Rings Online! It is a Minas Tirith of games.

2)I dabbled in psychedelics back in the early 1980s...

You made a laugh escape me when I read this! Oh to have been a crow in the Pacific Northwest in 1982 to have perhaps seen such sights...

You give good advice about drugs. Rubeus doesn't play for free.

Saturn's Pet

replies.title: Re: Psychedelics
replies.content:
What about societies in which etheogens have an established historical usage and are part of ceremonies which presumably "tighten the nuts"? There have been persistent theories that the Eluesenian rites, among others, included some chemical enhancement. Peyote rituals, ayahuasca (the real deal, not ceremonies patched together for the tourist trade) and others come to mind. It would seem to me that experienced guides, structure and the social support of a priesthood or other group would make such rituals a different experience from randomly dropping acid and watching the wallpaper move.

Rita R

replies.title: Re: Psychedelics
replies.content: If you belong to such a society, then you don't need to ask me. If you don't belong to such a society, my advice stands.

replies.title: Re: Drug use and magical practice
replies.content:
My experience with smoking marijuana in the late Sixties and early Seventies was helpful to me in readying me for magical practice in a couple of ways. It gave me the experience of several radically altered states which at that time I would not have otherwise experienced. It made me aware of the ways and the degree to which what I notice with my conscious mind is filtered by routines and algorithms that operate far below my conscious attention. Getting stoned also made me aware that the way I interpret sensory data is governed by habit, and that the habits can be changed intentionally.

A specific example of both the unconscious filters and the habitual interpretations is one that is familiar to every marijuana smoker: listening to a familiar piece of recorded music that you have heard many times while clean and sober. Many people who do this notice new things in the music, such as patterns and feelings, that they never got before. One may have the same experience of getting something new out of any familiar and habitual activity, such as washing dishes by hand, taking a favorite walk, doing a religious ritual, watching a movie you have seen before.

I became aware of the unconscious filters of incoming sensory data by getting stoned enough that walking became a bit more challenging than usual, and then noticing strong effects of time dilation and a different experience of how far away things in the immediate environment were.

To sum up, smoking marijuana gave me some direct experiences of the ways that my pictures of the world and reality are influenced by the way my mind and my brain usually operate, and that I can make reversible interventions in those routines which will then present me with different versions of reality.

In order to be useful as part of a magical practice, these realizations have to be more than intellectual concepts. One has to experience them directly. Marijuana smoking gave me direct experiences easily, without a lot of practice or preparation, and the insights stayed with me.

I don't think frequent marijuana smoking did me any good at all, and like many people I know, I have not smoked frequently since the end of the Seventies. Nowadays it is possible to get medical marijuana products that contain only the active ingredients which have the effects you want, whether it be pain relief, prevention of muscle spasms, or some mental effect.

People who have a regular meditation practice say that meditation is safer and gets you farther than psychedelic drugs. My magical practice involves spending time in natural environments such as sitting by a creek, and rituals that engage my senses and that include physical movement. I also get some consciousness alteration from basic meditations such as grounding, centering, banishing and clearing my aura. I have learned to become a little more aware of the energies, thoughts and feelings of some of the places and beings in natural and built environments.

.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Can the Middle Pillar be done astrally, like after an astral pentagram ritual? If so how would you do it-- would you picture the energy centers in your physical body and your imaginal body at The same time?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes and yes. I do this fairly often while traveling, or in other settings where it won't work to do the ritual physically.

comments.title: Finding lost object
comments.content:
(Tolkienguy)

What would be a good way (usable by someone with no magical training) to find a lost object? My car key snapped off its fob several weeks ago and dissapeared, and the dealership is insisting I have the car towed to them before they'll do anything.

replies.title: Re: Finding lost object
replies.content:
Um, that's a little like asking what would be a good way (usable by someone with no surgical training) to remove a gall bladder. If you don't have the training, there are things you simply can't do.

You might want to try contacting a locksmith, though. They have the necessary tools to pop open a locked car, and may even be able to rekey it for you; what's more, locksmiths usually do house calls. Be prepared to prove that you own the car, though!

replies.title: Re: Finding lost object
replies.content:
I worked for a picture framing shop which was owned by devout Catholics. There were five of us employees and we were always misplacing some little tool as we moved around the frame shop. The owners taught us their prayer to St. Anthony, and it worked! Pretty much every time, within a few minutes, we would run across the lost pliers, wire or whatever,

The prayer went something like this...
St. Anthony, St. Anothy,
Pleas look around.
Something's lost
and must be found!

replies.title: Re: Finding lost object
replies.content:
Good Saint Anthony, come around
Something’s lost that must be found!

comments.title: Animation, et al
comments.content:
Sorry you’re feeling morose. Dion Fortune looks a bit that way too - she appears *very* serious-minded indeed.

I want to correct a mistaken impression I seem to have given last week. I am *not* doing a mashup of Golden Dawn with the Druid Magic Handbook. I’m working with Hellenic deities using Golden Dawn structures, informed by material from the Celtic Golden Dawn (your elemental pentagrams there are *far* easier to remember than the GD ones).

I’ve given some thought to the question I raised last week, which has been on my mind for a long time, and have decided that I’ll go ahead with creating the elements of the temple I’m contemplating, one at a time. However the images I have in mind will be devotional only; I will not attempt to animate them for the time being. One writer on magic with whom I’m acquainted suggested that such images might, after much ritual use, tend to become ensouled; I don’t think I have a problem with that.

This is a long term project. I’ll start with a layout for the floor design, which I think you may have seen:

https://instagram.com/p/BRJ7E9PghH_/

It’ll be a couple of meters in diameter. The ideal material would be a high quality cast cement, or even cut stone; but building budgets being what they are, I’ll start with a simple painting on canvas drop cloth.

Kevin

replies.title: Re: Animation, et al
replies.content: I found your comment in last week's Magic Monday -- the one where you quoted John Opsopaus -- and I didn't find any reference there to your doing a mashup of GD and Druid Magic Handbook material. Was it perhaps an earlier conversation? With regard to your present comment, I think that's probably the better option. If appropriate entities descend into your magical images of their own accord, that's a lot less potentially problematic than it would be if you conjure them into the images, with all the same benefits.

replies.title: Re: Animation, et al
replies.content:
Thanks! It’s good to have your confirmation of that idea, which feels right to me somehow. Perhaps I’ll be ready for something like the Opening of the Mouth a few years down the road, or perhaps I’ll decide against it. I’m getting the feeling that it’s better not to rush into such things.

Kevin

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hey JMG
if people reincarnate after a month or so after death, then when someone tries to use mediumship to communicate with an ancestor that died decades ago who are they really talking too?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Most people don't reincarnate a month or so after death. It can take up to several months just to pass through the Second Death, the point at which you shed your etheric body, and most people spend at least a few years, and sometimes much more than that, between one life and the next.

That said, there are a lot of low-grade spirits out there who are perfectly willing to pretend to be your Aunt Mabel in a seance if it gets them a free lunch of life energy from the medium. Spiritualism is an extremely mixed bag, even when fraud isn't involved (and it very often is -- Lamar Keene's book The Psychic Mafia is a useful exposé on the subject).

replies.title: fraudulent mediums
replies.content:
Houdini, the famous magician, also spent a great deal of time exposing fraudulent mediums. His autobiography is an interesting read on the subject. However a recent book about Houdini, called _The Witch of Lime Street_ is tedious and overwritten, not worth one's time IMHO.

Rita R.

replies.title: etheric body
replies.content:
JMG, I own a few of your books on magic but not the lot. is there a particular book in which you explain the etheric body, the aura, and the other bodies? If that book also goes into the Four Worlds theory of manifestation or the four planes of which the Astral is the one bordering where we mostly live, or something similar, all the better.

I keep coming across these concepts about energy bodies and planes, but I don't remember ever reading a systematic explanation of how they interact or are generated. I get a better grasp of ideas if I'm presented with an explanation of the entire system together with examples of the details, and this stuff seems to be less abstract than the opening pages of The Cosmic Doctrine.

replies.title: Re: etheric body
replies.content: I haven't written such a book. I suppose that might be a project to consider once I get the current flurry of projects off my plate!

replies.title: Re: etheric body
replies.content:
For what it's worth, I found the brief explanation of the levels of being in JMG's Monsters book useful.
Jonathan.

comments.title: Is money a form of magic?
comments.content:
The anarchist writer Hakim Bey, also known as Peter Lamborn Wilson, has written that minted money, once invented, proved to be a highly successful form of magic, perhaps the most successful ever.

The historian Morris Berman has written that he was present at the fall of the Berlin Wall, or very shortly thereafter. Near the newly breached Wall he encountered a table where Soviet artifacts were being offered, for sale or given away - I forget which. They were things like red badges and commissars’ hats, symbols of authority that until very recently had stricken fear into the hearts of a great many people. But now that the Soviet Union had fallen, wrote Berman, they were dead objects, empty symbols devoid of power.

I imagine that a devalued currency might be much like those objects, a symbol of value denuded of its once formidable potency, and now turned into a joke, like Confederate money. To what extent, if any, may our collective belief in the efficacy of money be considered a form of magic?

Kevin

replies.title: Re: Is money a form of magic?
replies.content: I'd describe it as a very debased sort of magic, the sort of cheap thaumaturgy that plays on the weaknesses of human thinking -- much like advertising in that. The composer Richard Wagner wrote a fascinating little essay in which he traced the legend of the Rhinegold, "the treasure of the waters," back to its primordial myth-origins and forward to a disastrous split, in which the spiritual content became embodied in mysticism and legends such as the Holy Grail, while its unspiritual side became embodied in money. I should probably do a post about that one of these days.

replies.title: Re: Is money a form of magic?
replies.content: That sounds like a very interesting post!

replies.title: Re: Is money a form of magic?
replies.content:
Yes you should definitely do that one of these days! I'm fascinated by the glamour (in the sense you use it) of money. We describe it as filthy and grubby, but we treat people who have piles of it as though they sparkle. And are wise.

I rarely comment on Magic Mondays because I have nothing to contribute, but it is consistently one of my favorite threads of the week and I really look forward to the discussion. Back to lurking.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
In the Encyclopedia of Natural Magic you said astrobiology showed how the planets affect things on earth. A quick search only showed stuff about the origin of life and the possibility of life on other planets. What sort of effects do the planets have on earth and what sources are ther on this?

When you said that to affect a large area with natural magic you would need resources similar to those that built Stonehenge, a thought jumped to mind what the modern version of that would look like. You could cook up large vats of infusions, load them into spray tanks on aircraft and disperse them over the target area. Only unlike Agent Orange it would make the crops flourish and the land and people prosper. Instead of chemtrails it would be alchemtrails. :)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I don't recall mentioning astrobiology at all in that book, and I don't find it in the index. I did talk quite a bit about astrology, and that's what you should look into if you want to know more about how the planets affect life on earth.

As for "alchemtrails," hah! I suppose that would be an option, but I prefer the old-fashioned approach -- you know, the one involving big standing stones and earthen mounds, establishing alignments along which the subtle energies of the land can flow. You could call them "alchemtrails," though the traditional term is "leys"...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The mention of astrobiology is in the section 'The Planetary Powers' on p24 of the 2017 printing.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Have you ever come across somebody who breaks astrology - you look at their chart and it just doesn't match the person you're looking at at all? Also, does astrology ever predict neurodiversity? For example does your chart in any way suggest that you would have Aspergers?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Not since I learned how to read a chart properly. It's very easy to find cases where a simplistic interpretation of the chart (say, just the sun sign) doesn't fit at all. As for neurodiversity in the chart, sure, though there's a lot of further research to be done on that subject, as in everything related to medical astrology. My chart has several placements in it that suggest a predisposition to some kind of chronic neurological disorder, though.

comments.title: Performance and Perversity
comments.content:
Hi JMG and readership,

If I may and this is an appropriate place to do so, I wish to check in about the essay I wrote about the aesthetic and ethical confusion creating perversity, which to be fair, had an occult dimension. Basically, I want to acknowledge that I lost steam on the project pretty quickly when: a) I saw clearly that it wasn't something I wanted to meditate on for an extended time, and b) I realized that I was attempting, essentially, to put the ideas of Transactional Analysis into more metaphorically opulent drag. Perversity, as I defined it, is precisely identical to crossed transactions and contamination of ego states. I'm not a fan of writing that says, "here are some concepts already presented, and now look, they are dressed up in different words!" which, with additional research, I saw I was doing. When I understood this more fully over the course of a few months, the urgency I had was lost and by then I had moved on to writing fiction. For whatever it's worth, on my end I'm glad I undertook the project; the practice of writing started me on to writing several pieces of fiction that I'm very happy with. Also, by virtue of the project, I learned more about TA which is, of course, very useful.

replies.title: Re: Performance and Perversity
replies.content: Fair enough.

comments.title: forgot to log in, posting this for additional clarity
comments.content:
The Performance and Perversity comment is mine. Also, for clarity's sake I feel I should offer a link to the essay: https://hitherandthitherwhenceandwhither.blogspot.com/2017/10/performance-and-perversity.html

Violet

replies.title: Re: forgot to log in, posting this for additional clarity
replies.content:
Violet,
I wonder if your essay wants to be more than one text. Alternately, the readers seem to be thrown by the discussion of transactional analysis. You seem to feel that you were rehashing TA, and in that sense not adding anything new to the conversation. And yet, the some readers don't appear to be highly familiar with TA, (I wasn't until recently) so perhaps the essay does have a place in the conversation. (Also, were you trying to get Mengle to speak like Dostoyevsky’s Grand Inquistitor-that might be a good angle.) I commented on your blog but I'm not sure if it took or not.

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content:
Good morning JMG,

I've some questions on your personal Geomancy practice:

1) Do you follow the lore when it comes to Rubeus or Cauda Draconis in the first house, I mean do you throw everything away or do you still read the Shield Chart?

2)If you still wanna go on and draw a House Chart anyway, do you opt for the Golden Dawn house layout where the 1st mother goes to the 10th house?
That is, provided the 2nd mother isn't either Rubeus or Cauda Draconis...

Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
1) I discuss this in my books on geomancy. The traditional lore regarding Rubeus and Cauda Draconis is far more subtle and useful than the simplistic version of it that found its way into the Golden Dawn papers. Rubeus in the first house means that your querent is lying to you -- if you're doing a chart for yourself, it means that you're not being honest with yourself. Cauda Draconis simply means that it's too late to do anything about the situation. Neither one prevents you from reading the chart.

2) I also discuss this in my books on geomancy. No, I do it the old-fashioned way -- the first mother goes in the first house, the second mother in the second house, and so on in order until the fourth niece (it's not a nephew in the medieval texts!) goes into the twelfth house.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I have a few questions about energy in magic and spirituality that may be related.

Galina Krasskova described going to a Pagan shop where several groups did rituals that siphoned energy out of the surrounding area leaving the plants sickly and dying. What sort of magic risks doing this?

What sort of imprint is left on an area where a massacre or other major act of violence has happened, and what is best to do about it?

Energy rituals like the Middle Pillar and Central Ray don't involve as much body movement as yoga and tai chi. Does this change the effect they have?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Good question. The kind I wouldn't want to practice, certainly! I'm not up on every kind of magic being practiced these days, and I'm not familiar with any magic that would do this, but Galina's more than capable enough to know what she's talking about and I see no reason to doubt her.

2) The same kind of imprint that's left on the psyche of someone who survives such an experience. The best thing to do would be to plant a lot of trees there and let the processes of nature dispell the horror over time/

3) There are plenty of varieties of yoga, and several forms of qigong, that don't involve movement either. Whether a working includes physical movement or not is a matter of subtle details of technique, based on exactly what effects you're trying to get.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I've recently done a trip to one of the really big, bloated cities our civilization builds, and I hated it. Yet, some part of me has this feeling that I need to move there. I'm not sure what it is, since the city has no obvious perks over where I live now, and plenty of obvious negatives.

The one possibility I can think of is that my higher self has something in mind it can best express there. Is this a possible explanation?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It might be that; it might also be that someone else wants you to move there and is thinking that thought at you. I'd encourage you to consider the matter very carefully and seek divination if possible.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I have relatives who live there, enjoy my company, and absolutely refuse to consider moving. They're rarely even willing to visit somewhere else. That may be the case then.

I'm going to perform divination once I have a better idea of what exactly I want to ask the oracle.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Cities seem to me to be the equivalent in societies of black holes in the cosmos. They attract everything (and everyone) in their neighborhood, they swallow it (and them) up, and nothing gets out once it's inside.

And people who live in them find it hard to understand why anyone would want to remain outside. They become part of the black hole of the city, and their desire for company is part of the city's "gravitational" force that seeks to pull others inside, too.

That said, do divination. Resist. Reduce visits steadily. As needed, move farther away and stay there.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: In the Galabes post 'What Magic Can't Do' you mentioned the concept of occult anatomy. What kind of anatomical and physiological changes does practising magic cause?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's a subject for an entire book, and before it could be written you'd need grant funding to do quite a range of studies. For the time being, you might consider reading Dion Fortune's The Circuit of Force and Manly P. Hall's The Occult Anatomy of Man.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: With that Buddhist statue that had been really well consecrated, what does it feel like to sense someone else's magic? Are there some types of magic that are obvious and others less so or undetectable?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's really hard to describe, as the English language is very poorly supplied with words to describe magical perceptions. Have you ever been in the presence of a human corpse, and sensed what a body is like when there's nobody there any more? This statue was alive. Yes, some types of magic are more detectable than others.

replies.title: sensing activated statues
replies.content:
When I was a young girl, our family took a vacation trip to San Francisco that included a visit to the Japanese Tea Garden in Golden Gate Park. This would have been in the late Fifties. There is a life sized bronze statue of the meditating Buddha right next to a path in the garden. We paused briefly to look at it and then my parents moved on and I had to follow them.

(Wikipedia tells me that the Gump family donated the statue to the Garden in 1949. I don't know how old it is. The garden itself dates to the 1894 World's Fair, and was developed and tended by the Hagiwara family until they were sent to an internment camp.)

I recognized the statue as a work of art from an Asian religion. My parents had previously taken me to the art museum at the Smithsonian, where I had seen lots of Christian works of art. The religion I was brought up in does not make images of deities. Nothing in my upbringing had exposed me to the idea that this object might be different from any other work of art.

In the minute or so that we stood looking at this Buddha, I experienced a feeling that it was radiating a powerful energy, not unbenign. I took note of the sensation. I said nothing to my parents, because what could I have said?

The next time I had an experience like this was when I was in my early twenties and had begun to practice magic and to learn how to sense energy in charged objects. The previous building housing the UC Berkeley Art Museum had just opened. (That building was torn down recently after standing about fifty years.) Among the various exhibitions was a statue that probably had been borrowed from the anthropological collection of the Lowie Museum. It was a life sized black basalt statue of the Egyptian lionheaded goddess Sekhmet. I've always been fond of cats and by this time I was a goddess worshipper, so of course I walked right up to Sekhmet to see whether I could commune with Her. I had a feeling that She was in the statue, but by this time I was predisposed to that possibility. I would have left an offering if I could have figured out a way to do it.

I subsequently learned that the ancient Egyptians had practices to ensoul deity statues in temples. I don't know whether Japanese Buddhists have such practices or whether the statue in the park had simply absorbed energy poured into it by prayer, like a battery. If the latter is the case, I think the battery may be running down from lack of recharge, based on later visits I have made to the Tea Garden.

My experience working with objects on an altar has led me to think that bronze and black basalt are ideal materials for absorbing and holding magical charges. The only intentional work of this kind that I have done has been to charge crystals. One of these was used as a focus in a major ritual; I kept this in a box and continue to use it from time to time. The other is a small crystal that I treat like erasable paper and clear after use. I've also got a ritual knife that seems to be getting more powerful from use--when I use it to trace a line or boundary, I see a line of energy coming out of the tip, which was not visible to me when the knife was newly consecrated.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Again on Uranus, if I may: As the strongest planet in my natal chart, in Scorpio in house ten almost conjunct Midheaven, I've found the effects of its move last month into Taurus in house four to have been rather startling and quite immediate, and the same has been true for a number of close friends whose Uranus also features prominently in their charts. In particular, over the last four weeks everything home-related has suddenly been thrown into question, and I still don't know where I'll be living and working come fall -- quite disconcerting for this homebody academic! Is this normally the case, whereby the stronger an outer planet is in one's chart, the more immediate the manifestation of its transit energies in one's personal (i.e., not just collective) life?

Further, on the theme of "the wise man rules his stars": Given how treacherous the academy is these days (and only getting worse!), especially for junior historians, for the last several years I've naturally been regularly checking in on my career trajectory by means of geomantic and astrological forecast; they have consistently pointed to this year or the next at the latest as being one of a major professional transition, involving a change of jobs and significantly increased pay and influence in my field. The same has been independently confirmed by a talented palmist and tarot reader respectively. With all occult-scientific indicators converging on this single theme, then, I've been mentally and emotionally preparing myself to make the leap (I really do love where I am and have precisely zero desire to move), though was still surprised at how quickly the ground has given way beneath me over the last four weeks. However, being in such a state of mental and emotional preparedness for major change has, it would appear, rather magically brought about an even more unexpected and delightful result: the opportunity to make this professional leap up right here at my current institutional home. Yet were I to go this route -- and it seems right and good and prudent that I do --, this would ostensibly contravene several years of remarkably consistent occult data.

Now the Persian grimoires I work on constantly repeat the maxim that one must know the future (divination) to change it (magic), though heaven knows I haven't consciously tried to do the latter in this case. Might this be the principle at play here nevertheless? Can mere inner preparation and explicit, conscious surrender to the fate-destiny process magically unlock new and unforeseeable possibilities in this fashion? More specifically, has geomancy, in your experience, ever told you what you needed to hear to guide you toward a specific course of action -- one that resulted in the invalidation of the reading?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) That varies immensely depending on the nature of the planet, the aspects it makes to your natal chart, what factors are in play in your progressed chart and your current transits, and so on.

2) How does it contravene any of that? You're still facing a major transition and a career boost; it's just that the journey is taking place in a slightly less material form.

3) Very much so. If a pattern needs to work itself out, it can do so on any plane, from the material to the spiritual. If you've already made the inner transition, the outer transition may not need to happen at all, or it may happen in a much less traumatic way. Remember that matter is the outer husk of spirit...

comments.title: Workplace Bullying Closure
comments.content:
I was wondering if there was any sort of ritual for someone that has no magical training at all to finalise or bring closure to the bullying and harassment I experienced at my last workplace.

I left almost 2 years ago after suffering from serious stress and depression. It was very destructive and insidious, so I didn't realise what was happening until I almost had a breakdown. Afterwards, I could not even think about doing the same job (engineering) as it maxe me feel sick, so whilst trying to recover (diagnosed as PTSD, so I've done therapy, read a lot of books on the subject and so forth) I have also started a career change. I feel much better now, most of the anger has subsided and it's only my confidence that is still missing somewhat, now that I have started to consider applying for jobs.

I kept some work clothes intending to burn them later on together with some paperwork, but wonder if there is something better to do with it in terms of a ritual?

Also, is there any way to know if there was foul play involved? My career and work relationships were going great until a young couple joined and they started to manipulate the higher ups and undermine me and my work. It was a small company, so I guess it was easy enough, but nobody really liked them the first few months, then all of the sudden things changed...

Thank you so much for your time!

replies.title: Re: Workplace Bullying Closure
replies.content:
A bonfire is a very good ritual approach to such things. Find a place where you can safely build a really big fire, well away from anything flammable. (You can do this all by yourself or with friends, depending on what's more comfortable for you.) Get plenty of wood, set the fire, and as you light it, say aloud, "This is a fire of cleansing, a fire of purification, a fire of endings. I light it with this intention."

Feed it with wood until you've got a good roaring fire. Then take the first of the items from your workplace you want to burn, and say, "This is the bullying I suffered at (name of workplace). I give it to the flames. Let it be consumed in the fire." Throw it in and let it burn.

Take the next item, and say, "This is the (whatever you want to say here) I suffered at (name of workplace). I give it to the flames. Let it be consumed in the fire." Throw it in and let it burn.

Repeat this with as many items as you want to burn, and as many bad experiences as you want to burn with them. Once you've run out of things to offer, sit or stand there facing the fire. Call to mind all the pain and misery and humiliation and wretchedness you felt at that workplace, and give those, too, to the fire to be burnt. Stay there until the flames have died down to coals, and then get water, lots of it, and extinguish the fire, drowning every ember. As you begin pouring the water, say, "The fire is done. Its work is accomplished. What is burnt is behind me now." Then go home by a different route than you came, and bathe in water that contains a pinch of salt, a pinch of hot pepper, and a splash of vinegar. That completes the ritual.

As for determining if there was foul play involved, that would take either a good private investigator or an equally competent diviner. If you know one of those, talk to them.

comments.title: Tracing the 3 rays
comments.content:
Greetings, Regarding the CGD, I notice that the rays are traced in different order when purifying (evoking form) & consecrating (invoking form) the temple in the Ovate Grade, in comparison to the way it is instructed for the OIW Ritual.

1. Am I correct in noticing this difference?

2. If so, is this difference important/significant?

Thank you

replies.title: Re: Tracing the 3 rays
replies.content: Good. Try changing the way you trace them in each ritual and see if you notice the difference.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I was wondering if you had an opinion of John and Caitlin Mathews work? A broad question I realize as there's quite a lot of it...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I haven't kept up with their work, so don't have an overall opinion. The material of theirs I read early on -- primarily The Western Way and some of John Matthews' Grail material -- left me unimpressed, but that may simply have been a difference in approaches.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,
I can’t believe I’m writing over such a ridiculous little issue, but I did learn of it from you, so here goes:

I wish to adopt the polytheist version of the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram that you described on Jan 15, 2018.

You mentioned that the names of each of the four deities should have four and only four letters in their spellings (at least if you are interested in best results). Does this 4-letter rule also apply to the patron deity? What does one do if there are no such spellings? Can you make up your own? Could/should one adopt a different alphabet (the Greek spellings are no help, and I’m considering the Coelbren) and contrive a 4-letter spelling?

Hermes is my patron, I will be working with the ancient Greek Pantheon, and the only deities with four-letter spellings are Zeus, Ares and Hera. I feel that Poseidon, Athena and Hestia (with Zeus of course) would be appropriate for my LRP. What do you suggest?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
First, when I say that the names of the deities in the quarters should have four and only four letters, that applies only to the name of the deities in the four quarters. If I'd wanted to extend that restriction to the patron deity I would have said so.

Second, as for deities with names of four letters in the Greek pantheon, come on! I'm not even a Hellenic Pagan, and I know better than that. You could add Gaia to your three and have a good set, or Rhea, or Tyche -- the "ch" is one letter in Greek, after all. You could swap out Ares with Eros, for that mater. You could use Hades (four letters in Greek), or Aion, or -- well, you can look up the others yourself. It really isn't that hard.

comments.title: Phosphenism
comments.content:
Another subject if I may,

What is your opinion on Francis Lefebure’s Phosphenism?

What about the affirmations below?

“...In Orthodox worship, the priest sways while praying and focusing on a candle. Clairvoyants also use that rudimentary and instinctive practice when they place a candle near a crystal ball. Again, they obtain a phosphene using the reflection of light, the ball acting as a magnifying glass. As all reflected light is more or less polarized, that explains the origin of flashes of clairvoyance. The ‟magical mirrors” are absolutely not magical: it is their capacity for reflection that matters...”

“...Nostradamus used the reflection of the moon on a silver tray, when he wanted to provoke phenomena of clairvoyance and transmit them to Catherine of Medici...”

replies.title: Re: Phosphenism
replies.content: I'm not familiar with Lefebure's ideas so have no opinion on them at all. As for the quotes, yes, there are techniques like that used by some schools to develop clairvoyance; other schools, including the one I trained in, use techniques that don't involve reflective objects at all.

replies.title: Re: Phosphenism
replies.content: Lefebure is simply mistaken about Orthodox Christian worship; what he describes is far from being standard priestly practice.  (It's not my religion, but the history and practice of Orthodox Christian liturgies have of necessity been part of my scholarly equipment as a Medieval Slavic philologist.)

comments.title: Amulets
comments.content:
With regard to amulets, how long do the effects of an amulet tend to last? If it’s one that is worn on the person, should it be worn daily or only when in a situation that would call for the amulet? If an amulet is not being worn, where is a good place to keep it? Once an amulet is no longer needed, or if its effects have diminished to the point of no longer being useful, what should be done with it? Should it be burned, buried, taken apart ceremoniously? Thank you for any information about the handling of amulets. I have not made one yet but am planning to put one together soon.
Thank you,
Yanocoches in Colorado

replies.title: Re: Amulets
replies.content: You're asking for general rules in a subject where there are only specific recipes, and a lot of varying opinions. I'd encourage you to pick up a few good books on making amulets, follow the directions, experiment with the results, and develop your own set of habits for working with them -- that's what everyone does sooner or later anyway.

replies.title: Re: Amulets
replies.content:
In traditional Judaism there is a rule that any piece of paper or parchment on which is written the name of God in Hebrew letters must be preserved, even if it is a mere scrap.

Anyone who creates an amulet containing a divine name written in the Hebrew alphabet might want to bear that in mind.

comments.title: Omens
comments.content: Do you have any book recommendations concerning the interpretation and meanings of omens? Perhaps you have even written such a book yourself?

replies.title: Re: Omens
replies.content: I haven't written such a book, and don't happen to know of any books on the subject. Does anyone else have any suggestions?

replies.title: Re: Omens
replies.content:
I learned to read omens by accident. It changed the trajectory of my life. Here’s what I recommend:

Get a good book on dream interpretation. It should have two main sections (or you may need two books) one is a dictionary of symbolism and the other is a bunch of techniques and how-to information that start with “how to remember your dreams” and go on from there in complexity, it will teach you how to interpret dreams. I used The Everything Dreams Book by Trish and Rob MacGregor, but I’m sure there are a lot of books that will work just as well. (and if you have trouble remembering dreams, read your dream journal before you go to sleep. If you don’t have ANY of your own dreams, read the sample dreams in the book. )

When you have learned how to interpret dreams, you will know how to read the symbolism in anything: Tarot, movies, religious rites, commercials, and, most importantly for you, omens and auguries. You will recognize an omen by the weirdness of the event combined with its timing and a weird sort of recognition. I usually feel it like a gut certainty but you will learn your own sensation. When an omen happens you just interpret the event the same way you would if it had happened in a dream. If you want to specialize in types of omens like augury (bird omens) then you do some extra study on the symbolism of birds. Learning dream interpretation was a lot of work and it took a lot of effort but the skill has paid off immensely in positive changes in my life and in omens foretelling major life events (and in learning my personality flaws as well).

replies.title: Re: Omens
replies.content: Thanks!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
John--

In the DA construct, we speak of the seven grades/levels/mysteries, broken generally (?) into the first four and last three. In my attempt to better understand the structures involved, a couple of intertwining questions re this:

1) Is this notion of "4-and-3" division common to seven-fold esoteric systems? (This question is more out of curiosity than anything else.)

2) I noticed that the structure of the language changes at the Fourth Cantref -- namely, the banishing aspect excludes the component relating to a specific unbalanced expression (e.g. "inappropriate thoughts" in the First, or "misguided intentions" in the Third) and that the verbiage alters to "unbalanced manifestations." I recall that Earth is acts as something of a bridge between the first three Cantrefs and the latter three, but is there a specific rationale behind the alteration in the form/language here?

3) Finally, the latter three, I noticed, have no banishing component. Is it that the manifestations of Spirit (Below, Above, Within) cannot be unbalanced and hence there is no need for banishing?

Again, thank you for this regular Q&A forum.

David, by the lake

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, yes, and yes. ;-)

comments.title: Banishing while Jewish
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

About a year and a half ago I began a daily meditation and divination practice. I did not intend to practice ritual magic, but figured that a daily banishing would be a good idea, too. As I am an observant Jew, I assumed the Kabbalistic Cross would be most appropriate. But every version I found seemed somehow weirdly Christianized and felt bad to me. Eventually I adapted it, substituting some more familiar Jewish names chosen pretty randomly and a phrase from the Amidah, a foundational prayer in the daily liturgy, for the opening and closing phrase. And I used this for a year and a half. It helped me focus and clear my mind and that was all I needed.

Until a few months ago, when I started having disturbing dreams that suggested I needed a better banishing ritual.

I looked at the Sphere of Protection, but was stymied by the age and gender requirements for the divine names. Some Hebrew names for the divine are grammatically plural or feminine, but I had always been instructed as a child that that was meaningless. A learned friend of mine suggested I read Aryeh Kaplan’s ‘Meditation and Kabbalah’ for more background and perhaps inspiration.

But instead of inspiration, as I read it I realized that the foundations of Kabbalah are rooted in the same biophobic milieu as early Christianity, and it was this that made the Kabbalistic cross seem so icky to me. The idealization of spirit and rejection of matter is not a good mode for my practice, it seems.

I tried meditating on this several times and it messed terribly with me—kept getting very upset, couldn’t do my divinations, had a general crisis of faith, etc.

You always suggest that a person who has a good relationship with a particular deity stick with that deity in one’s magical practice. And I do still feel that I have a good relationship with the Jewish god…but it’s become obvious that the Kabbalah is not good for me or my relationship with that god.

So I have questions for you, and for any readers who might have insight to share:

1. What are the Jewish names (if any) for the male and female aspects of god that are typically used in the SoP?

2. Would it be appropriate to just use the Druid names suggested by AODA and consider those gods and goddesses manifestations of the divine, and thus not in conflict with my faith? (The SoP, and Druid names in particular, appeal to me because of the embrace of Earth and matter as being just as important as spirit.)

3. Is there another banishing ritual I should consider instead? Ex. my same learned friend who recommended I read Kaplan has translated a Jewish grimoire and sent me a banishing ritual he found in it to cleanse a house of a demon, thinking perhaps that was what I needed…but it was very much along the lines of natural magic. But perhaps natural magic would be a good body of knowledge to cultivate instead of a banishing ritual if I am not planning on practicing any additional ritual magic?

Thank you for your patience and any insight you may share.

--Ms. Krieger

replies.title: Re: Banishing while Jewish
replies.content: You might consider calling on angels during the Sphere of Protection as one option, which would let you draw on Kabbalistic tradition without directly invoking divine names. JMG answered a question March 19th on here about the angels to use in the elemental gate portion - I'm not sure who you would call on for the elemental cross.

replies.title: Re: Banishing while Jewish
replies.content:
The Jewish version of the SoP uses the six three-lettered names from the first chapter of the Sepher Yetzirah, which dates from long before the Qabalah (which, as Gershom Scholem demonstrated many years ago, was a Jewish reworking of Gnosticism, and thus embraced the Gnostic biophobia). Here's the relevant passage in William Wynn Westcott's translation:

"11. He selected three letters from the simple ones, and sealed them as forming his great Name, I H V and he sealed the universe in six directions.
Five.- He looked above, and sealed the height, with I H V.
Six.- He looked below, and sealed the deep, with I V H.
Seven.- He looked forward, and sealed the East, with H I V.
Eight.-He looked backward, and sealed the West, with V H I.
Nine.- He looked to the right, and sealed the South, with V I H.
Ten.-He looked to the left, and sealed the North, with H V I."

(Of course those names are properly Yod Heh Vav and so on.)

The Elemental Cross, using the same instructions from the Sepher Yetzirah, is done as follows:

Face east with your hands at your sides. Raise your hands up in an arc and join them above your head, palm to palm, as you visualize a beam of light descending from infinite space to a point above your head. Draw your hands down to your forehead, and say, "One is the Spirit of the Living God." (You can translate this into Hebrew if you wish.)

Draw your hands down to the level of your heart and visualize a ray of light descending into infinite space below you. Say: "From the spirit He made air."

Extend your right hand out to your right, and visualize a beam of light going the same direction. Say: "From the air He made water."

Extend your left hand the same way, and visualize a beam of light going the same direction into infinite space. Say: "From the water He made fire."

Now cross your arms, right over left, and visualize beams of light going into infinite space before and behind you. Say: "Blessed be the Lord God of Israel, Amen." Then go on with the rest of the ritual, using the six names already given.

That's what I would recommend -- but it occurs to me that if you have a good relationship with the god of Israel, why not ask him? Prayer is always a good idea when considering a working of this sort; ask for advice, and listen for the response.

replies.title: Re: Banishing while Jewish
replies.content: Thank you very much for your detailed response, and to daveotn as well.

comments.title: when to practice what
comments.content:
JMG,

Hi. Over the past few months I have purchased your books: Learning Ritual Magic, Druidry Handbook, Druidry Magic Handbook and The Celtic Golden Dawn. I had been reading your books on peak oil, industrial decline, etc for quite some time now, started to read your blog last year, began to notice quite a few blog entries (terms, concepts, etc) that were completely new to me, and eventually figured out that you offer a whole separate stream of knowledge related to authentic (mature) occult magic. Well, I surprisingly found myself being drawn to this and so have begun to explore it. I gave the above books a general reading so I could get a felt flavor for this new arena, and then started to practice various rituals which you outline in the books.

In particular, I started with the Lessor Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram, as laid out in The Celtic Golden Dawn. I was practicing that for a few weeks when I found myself ‘drawn’ to The Sphere of Protection as laid out in the Druidry Magic Handbook. I have been practicing the SOP for several months now and getting quite comfortable with it. And, I also have a desire to become more proficient at the LBRP. And, of course I have noticed other ritual through the books that are fascinating and useful as well. I suppose my question is how to best coordinate the various magical rituals, when to practice one as opposed to an another, etc, do any of them conflict with one another…..thank you for answering this question. I am finding this entry into ritual magic quite powerful and useful in my life just now.

replies.title: Re: when to practice what
replies.content: I very strongly recommend choosing one system to learn at a time. Pick one book of instruction -- it doesn't matter which -- and complete the whole set of training in it. Then go on to the next one you want to do. It's tempting to jump from this to that to the other thing, but that's the way of the dabbler -- and trying to learn more than one system at a time leads promptly to burnout. Slow and steady wins the race!

comments.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)

2) A very complex cat, worth reading but not to be taken uncritically.

comments.title: reincarnation
comments.content:
JMG-

I have a question about reincarnation and past lives, I've been practicing the meditations in LRM for about 8 months now and just in the last week interesting images have started appearing to me, not while meditating, but at other moments throughout the day. They're never concrete, not like memories from childhood say, just a flash of an image accompanied by a very strong emotion (both times it was a very visceral fear). The strange thing is they're very concrete images, but nothing I've ever experienced, the image and the emotion feel foreign to me in a way nothing else ever has. I'm just curious is this could be something coming up from a past experience.

And, partly related, could you recommend a good book on past life memories, anecdotal or otherwise? (I've read William Walker Atkinson's reincarnation and the law of karma but that's about it)

replies.title: Re: reincarnation
replies.content: Might be past life memories, might be any number of other things. Keep doing your practices, and see what happens. As for a book on reincarnation, I highly recommend Ian Stevenson's works, especially Twenty Cases Suggestive of Reincarnation.

comments.title: a little aside
comments.content:
Archdruid,

This question is a bit off the beaten track of my normal line of inquiry, but the topic caught my attention.

What exactly is a servitor? Is it actually a thing? Is it useful or dangerous?

Regards,

Syfen

replies.title: Re: a little aside
replies.content: I was going to ask JMG something similar. There is currently an entire subculture of mostly young people called "tulpamancers" who are into such things. To me, it sounds like...I don´t know...schizophrenia?

replies.title: Re: a little aside
replies.content:
I've spent some time browsing the "tulpamancy" communities, mostly out of curiosity, so I might be able to address your questions.

Schizophrenia is a common first impression of the practice but people more acquainted with it are more likely to compare it with Dissociative Identity Disorder.

replies.title: Re: a little aside
replies.content: It's what a slightly older generation of occultists call an artificial elemental: a detached lump of your own life energy charged with some task or purpose. Yes, they exist, though there are many other ways to do magic, and in my experience, some of the other ways are noticeably more effective and have fewer downsides. Still, some people apparently get good results with them.

replies.title: Re: a little aside
replies.content: Interesting, I guess I'll put it on my get-to list. I have enough on my plate with the current system, and philosophy that I'm learning. One system at a time, as you say.

comments.title: Christian beings and the Druid calendar
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

With Alban Heruin coming up I have a question for you. I was using the guidelines in the Druidry Handbook regarding who to invoke, as a Christian Druid, during my seasonal celebrations (on page 80) but they seem like they might be a little off - it lists the archangel Raphael on two consecutive days, for instance, which seems like it could have been a typesetting error.

Having since gotten the Druid Magic Handbook it lists a set of days more closely mapped to the Druid wheel of the year, under the table for Stone Circle Rituals (p. 159). This also corresponds better with the invocations I use in the Sphere of Protection, and the elemental correspondences thereof - i.e., Michael matches fire and the summer solstice, Raphael matches Air and the spring equinox, etc. It does, unfortunately though, push St. Michael away from Michaelmas.

Any recommendation on which of these two is more appropriate for an AODA member doing seasonal rituals? I realize neither one is exactly right (one being for a slightly different calendar, and the other for a different type but related ceremony)?

replies.title: Re: Christian beings and the Druid calendar
replies.content: I'd encourage you to pray about it and make your own choice! Druidry being Druidry, there's no such thing as one right way, and as a Christian Druid, it's just as much your choice which archangel to invoke at a given season as it would be a Pagan Druid's choice which god to invoke,

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG
If the god in the golden dawn rituals is the Jude’s-Christian god, then why are Egyptian gods still used the the rituals as well? Aren’t they enemies?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The classic Golden Dawn workings use Egyptian godforms -- the astral images of the Egyptian gods -- but don't actually pray to or invoke the presence of the Egyptian deities.

comments.title: Children and Magic
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid,

You’ve answered many of these questions before, but kindly let me know whether I recall this correctly:

Natural Magic is safe around children. Intense ritual magic shouldn’t be practiced at all by those with children until the children are three. It shouldn’t be practiced in the house until the children are seven. The SOP is generally safe to do around children of any age. Children should be out of puberty before they start practicing intense ritual magic themselves (15/16+).

Then for my questions: From what age can an interested child learn the SOP themselves? And meditation? At what age would the Druid Egg be appropriate (and other forms of basic self-energy work)? These things would be very useful for highly sensitive children.

Yours under the Sacred Oaks,
Brigyn

replies.title: Re: Children and Magic
replies.content: The Druid Egg and similar very simple workings can be taught to children any time after around the age of seven. I'd leave the SoP until they're twelve or so. Meditation is fine at any age.

comments.title: Banishing before meditation
comments.content:
Hi JMG! Thank you for this forum.

You mentioned before that before meditation you should definitely do a banishing ritual like the SoP. But you've also mentioned that a person should really only banish once a day. Due to my schedule I make an effort to do the SoP first thing in the morning. But I meditate in the evening after putting my child to bed as I am not rushed and can take my time to concentrate.

Should I therefore do 2 SoPs a day as i am currently doiqng or perhaps do a different banishing than the SoP before meditation? Or is once a day with the evening meditation the best bet?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Banishing before meditation
replies.content: I'd say try the different options and see what works best for you. So long as you're doing at least one banishing a day, and one session of meditation a day, you've got the basics covered; the rest is a matter of finding a fit between what works for you and what your schedule will permit.

comments.title: Not really a question but it was an interesting synchronicity
comments.content:
Yesterday morning I went with my roommate to get some Pan Dulce at the corner store, to get ready for the Germany-Mexico football match that we were gonna see at my house. While we were waiting to get dispatched, a lady walked by with her two dogs, one of which was a beautiful crossbreed. The dog stopped to smell my roomie and I took my chance to pet it. The lady told me his name was 'chucky', so I greeted the dog and they left. Then I turned around to pay the bread and the girl of the store had a t-shirt with Chucky, the evil doll from the movie. My friend and I just noted the curious coincidence and went back home.

We sat down to watch the first ever Germany-Mexico match, hoping for an honorable defeat at best. However, thanks to Hirving "Chucky" Lozano's goal, our team managed to defeat the champion of the last world cup.

I am still puzzled.

replies.title: Re: Not really a question but it was an interesting synchronicity
replies.content: Synchronicity happens. ;-)

comments.title: Walking meditation
comments.content:

Many thanks,

Morfran.

replies.title: Re: Walking meditation
replies.content: Experiment with them and see how they work for you. If I were rewriting that book today, I'd include more flexibility in the instructions for meditation.

comments.title: (no subject)

Bonnie

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't know whether grocery store beans will sprout or not! At the time I was developing that meditation -- I always do any practice I put in a book, so that I can advise people about it on the basis of experience -- I had a garden and was growing a lot of beans, so it wasn't an issue.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:

If it doesn't sprout in a reasonable amount of time, I may have a few floral individuals in the yard that have seeds large enough to be viewed well while sprouting.

Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Go ahead and try it.  It's been my experience that any dried seeds that are intact will usually germinate - maybe not as well as certified seed but well enough for non-commercial gardening or farming.  I've had good luck with beans, lentils, and quinoa.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hi!!

Try sprouting some green lentils or chickpeas.

comments.title: Conversion of Picts
comments.content:
JMG:

In your book The Celtic Golden Dawn you wrote that the last old druids might have went to the Picts to work against their conversion to Christianity.

Lately I've wondered why they did that given what I think would have been their labors' likely outcomes in these cosmic circumstances.

We weren't there (Or were we? Lol.) so we can only speculate about their motives and possibilities for purposeful action.

Nonetheless I wonder: why do you think they did so?

Saturn's Pet

replies.title: Re: Conversion of Picts
replies.content: They didn't go to the Picts; they were Picts. The Picts were a Celtic people speaking a language related to Welsh, Cornish and Breton -- that's why you find Brythonic roots like aber- ("river mouth") in Scottish place names such as Aberdeen, along with the equivalent Goidelic root inver- (which means exactly the same thing) in place names like Inverness. As Celts, the Picts had Druids, and those Druids tried to convince their fellow-Picts not to convert to Christianity.

replies.title: Re: Conversion of Picts
replies.content:
JMG:

You wrote that they last appeared among those Picts. I see now. I am contentedly corrected!

I of course imagined aging druids taking secretive and solemn road trips from Ireland and Wales to Pictish lands for an Alamo. Ha!

Now I can better explore both nighttime and daytime dreams I have of fighting beetle-clad Romans in foggy pines. Of course they resisted conversion!

Have you dreamed about Lovecraft yet while living in Providence?

Saturn's Pet

comments.title: Animal souls
comments.content:
I once chatted with a "therian", a person who claimed to be an animal born into a human body, in his case a wolf. There is an entire subculture of mostly young kids who make such claims, but this person seemed more serious, more on the Neo-Pagan/shamanistic side. My question though is if you believe it´s possible at all for a wolf to be reborn as a human, without loosing his wolf identity in the process? Dion Fortune mentions a therian-like human in her detective novel, which is supposed to be based on real cases, but in her scenario, the human evolves (or devolves?) into an actual physical animal! The notion of a wolf-spirit being born into a human body seems illogical to me, even from a spiritual viewpoint - it implies that the "astral" body is that of a wolf, and the physical (and etheric?) that of a human. Was this person just delusional, or was he somehow possessed by a wolf spirit, or his own previous self?

Tidlösa

replies.title: Re: Animal souls
replies.content: My take is that at the present time, since there are so many human bodies being born and such sharp decreases in the number of other large mammals, a lot of souls that haven't finished learning the lessons of other life forms and aren't really ready to become human yet are being born as humans anyway, because that's what's available. If they have unfinished business as animals, they're likely to get past life memories of their last animal existence, and given the level of esoteric ignorance common in today's society, going from that to "I must be a wolf" is easy enough. Of course there are a lot of other people who simply want to be something other than bored middle-class Americans, and pretending to be a wolf has become more fashionable these days in some circles than pretending to be Native American or African-American (cough, cough, Rachel Dolezal, cough cough).

comments.title: Elemental relationships
comments.content: If one wishes to use the SOP to banish laziness and procrastination, which would be the elementas to be used for banishing, as well as invoking the positive aspect?

replies.title: Re: Elemental relationships
replies.content: When you use the SoP for practical magic in that way, you invoke what you want from all seven elements, and banish what you don't want with all four material elements.

I usually do not get around to reading all the questions until Tuesday. I often post responses late on Tuesday. Last Monday's questions had four Tuesday responses from me, all listed as Anonymous, with (Deborah Bender) at the head of the actual post. eldriwolf also posted a late reply, and there may have been others.

If you post a question on Monday and you are interested in reading responses from others besides JMG, check back after Monday and you may pick up some stragglers.

Hi,

Happy Monday!

In the Druidry Handbook, you give instructions for an exercise that works with the nwyfre of trees. Can this exercise also be used with a body of water, or soil?

Thank you for your time.

comments.title: The marriage of Jesus
comments.content: This is not necessarily about occultism, but there are certainly tie-ins to the alternative history milieu, etc. Do you have any idea where the idea that Jesus was married to Mary Magdalene originally comes from? It was popularized by Dan Brown´s "The Da Vinci Code" based on "Holy Blood, Holy Grail" by the Three Non-Initiates, but when did the notion first appear? I read somewhere that the Mormon Church during its polygamous period (that would have been mid to late 19th century) believed that Jesus was married and had three wives, among them Mary Magdalene (the two others were Martha and the Other Mary).Joseph Smith was inspired by esotericism, so could the notion come from some esotericist tradition?

replies.title: Re: The marriage of Jesus
replies.content:
I'm not at all sure where the notion of a marriage between Jesus and Mary of Magdala came from, though it appears to go back quite a long ways in Gnostic writings. It's a pretty obvious thing to claim if you want to challenge the notion of Jesus as celibate, and since nobody knows either way, divine (and, ahem, less than divine) revelations on the subject face little risk of contradiction.

Did you know that in medieval European art, Mary Magdalene was routinely portrayed as a woodwose (basically, a medieval sasquatch), covered with fur? I've occasionally thought of manufacturing a conspiracy theory around that, with Mary Magdalene as the apostle to the yetis... ;-)

replies.title: Re: The marriage of Jesus
replies.content: I saw a limewood sculpture of hairy Mary and was wondering why she was portrayed as Our Lady of the Missing Link. :)

replies.title: Re: The marriage of Jesus
replies.content: The original "Hairy Mary" was Mary of Egypt, on whom there is a reasonably accurate Wikipedia article.  In the West, she got confused or conflated with Mary Magdalene (who had already been conflated with the "woman taken in adultery" who is the subject of a Gospel pericope).  Both were often depicted as a woman clothed only in her own very long hair.

replies.title: Re: The marriage of Jesus
replies.content:
Okay, that would explain a thing or two. I still find it amusing to imagine a Christian heresy in which sasquatches are the descendants of Jesus and Mary Magdalene, who was of course a Jewish yeti.

The madcap SF author Somtow Sucharitkul did a series of tales about the Roman conquest of America, in which the sasquatches turned out to be the Lost Tribes of Israel, so there's even a precedent. Should I hang out my shingle as a heresiarch? ;-)

replies.title: Re: The marriage of Jesus
replies.content:
I had thought you hung that shingle out years ago. It's a very good shingle; our society badly needs its heresiarchs.

I must look into Somtow Sucharitkul! It sounds like a delightful romp. Shades of one Charles A. L. Totten, and even Paul Foster Case!

replies.title: Re: The marriage of Jesus
replies.content: The stories have been collected into a volume, The Aquiliad -- Aquila is one of the characters, an ally of the Romans from the, ahem, Lacotii... I don't know if it's still in print, but it ought to be readily available on the used book market.

replies.title: Re: The marriage of Jesus
replies.content: I'm ordering a copy of that tonight! Thanks!

replies.title: Re: The marriage of Jesus
replies.content:
I've been told by an Orthodox Jew that of course Jesus was married, and his was the water-into-wine wedding in Canaan, as it is customary for the groom to provide the feast and drink for his wedding (hence his mother coming to him about running out of wine). I was also told that his disciples would not have called him "Rabbi" unless he was married, also, and that Mary Magdalene would not have traveled with the group extensively unless she was married to one of them. My source mentioned that all of this is glaringly obvious to anyone familiar with Jewish custom of the time, so it probably never occurred to any of the apostles to write that down, because most never intended to take it out of its cultural context.

This is secondhand knowledge, so if I am mistaken I will welcome correction. (I know you have at least a couple Jewish readers here.)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
In the Jyotish astrological tradition, it is said to be extremely bad karma for an astrologer to give an interpretation to someone when they aren’t 100% sure of what they are saying—this is said to come back on a practitioner as ill fortune.

Not completely differently, woe unto the person who represents themselves as a lineage holder in a Buddhist tradition or otherwise a teacher of Buddhism who transmits knowledge which is contrary to the teachings themselves. There are even “dharma protectors” (Dharmapala) who could drive such a person insane in the protectors’ quest to shield the dharma from corruption. Upon death, such a nefarious teacher AND their students are said to go to Buddhist “hell” (which is not like the Abrahamic versions, but hell from a Buddhist perspective), e.g., such a teacher may never in a million lifetimes even hear word of the dharma, and continue to cycle aimlessly through samsaric existence.

Are there any such warnings to students/teachers in any of the western esoteric traditions? Or is it the wild, wooly west? I know the adage “do what ye will and harm none” (Wiccan, I believe?) but who defines harm? Who cares, so to speak?

Thank you kindly!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Magical ethics are a tangled mess in the western occult tradition these days. The better grade of occult teacher back in the day had a lot to say about ethics, and about the way that mistreatment of others would circle back and clobber you, but a lot of what we see nowadays in the scene either ignores ethics altogether or uses them as an excuse to denounce the mote in their brother's eye while ignoring the beam in their own. Treat it as the wild, woolly west, check every teacher's words against their behavior, and make your own judgment.

replies.title: magical ethics in the West
replies.content:
I consider the ethics taught in Wicca to be decent but rather simplistic. I'm comparing them with my familial religion, which has a long and unbroken tradition of obsessive ethical examination, through which its teachers have had the opportunity to consider a lot of difficult situations.

IMO the comparatively undeveloped state of Wiccan ethical thought was not much of a problem before Wicca became popularized. In the early years it was difficult for a seeker to find anybody who would admit to knowing anything about witchcraft. That gave the teacher opportunities to check the potential student's character and values without the student being aware he was being evaluated. In those days, or so I'm told, the secrets carefully kept from outsiders were not the details of rituals; they were techniques for doing practical magic. Students received guidelines and the warnings, then were tested on self-examination and impulse control, and only then were they taught how to do spells.

This is of course an idealized description of what went on, but opening the gates and dismissing the gatekeepers does have some drawbacks.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
What is this practice of absorbing, or drawing energy from trees?

Is it for real? Does it do any good, any healing?

If so, are there any trees whose energies are not suitable or even deleterious for humans?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's called Tree Qigong in China, and there are many versions of it in other cultures. Yes, it works, and has various healthful effects. If you want to try it, I'd suggest reading this article for pointers.

comments.title: "Colorless" Light
comments.content: Regarding the ocean of colorless light in the instructions for the CGD Ovate meditations: is "colorless" another word for "white"?

replies.title: Re: "Colorless" Light
replies.content: No, it's another word for "clear." Is glass white?

replies.title: Re: "Colorless" Light
replies.content: Glass is not white, but it is also not luminescent, unless light of some color shines through it. When the student is instructed to visualize the colorless light increasing its intensity until it is as bright as the sun, is that not white? Or should I endeavor to keep it "clear" somehow (though I haven't yet figured out what that means)? I'm sorry if I'm seeming dense here.

comments.title: Asking about CosDoc intro
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
Just went through the Introduction to Cosmic Doctrine. In it the writer refers to more “evolved” humans as the Inner Plane Adepti. Is that being used as in has “progressed further” or “appropriately adapted”.
The whole introduction seems to have a rather elitist vibe, where the study of the doctrine smacked of something to increase spiritual status.
Probably just hit me the wrong way, but it stuck out partly due to the discussion you had on the blog recently regarding status seekers.

Thanks for any insight
Candace

replies.title: Re: Asking about CosDoc intro
replies.content:
You'll notice that I didn't assign any readings from the intro. That was written at a time when the concepts of evolution and progress were even more tangled than they are today, and occult writings in particular were awash in talk about "spiritual evolution" that meant "spiritual progress."

I encourage students these days to ignore the introduction -- especially if you've got the Millennium Edition -- and approach the images and ideas of the text on their own merits.

replies.title: Re: Asking about CosDoc intro
replies.content: Thank you!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I have 2 questions:

1) Last week, and in previous discussions, you mentioned that we have a higher/spiritual self that is more aware of the patterns that occur over our lifetimes , as well as the lessons that we're here to learn. My question is, how can one get in touch with their higher self, and suss out these patterns/lessons ?

2) How can one find/make a decent natal chart? Also, what is the best way to make use of a natal chart?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) You can't, and don't need to. Your higher self is already in as much touch with you as it needs to be, and will guide you as it usually does, by sending you challenges and opportunities. Your job as the lower self is to deal with what life brings you in as constructive, creative, and thoughtful a way as you can. Do that and you'll do fine.

2) You need the date, time, and place of your birth. Once you have those, go to any online horoscope website -- www.astro.com is the one I used back before I got my own astrology program -- enter those pieces of data, and hit the button. That'll get you your natal chart. Once you have it, get a few books on astrology from the library, and go through them, looking up what each of your placements (where the planets are, what's rising, etc.) in the books. That'll begin the process of teaching you how to use your natal chart.

KiwiJon
